title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita, 2022

Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and energy efficiency for the year 2022, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against energy intensity on a vertical axis measured in kilowatt-hours per dollar. The data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A broad negative correlation is visible across the distribution: as GDP per capita increases, the energy intensity generally decreases. Countries with the lowest GDP per capita, such as Burundi, Liberia, and Mozambique, cluster on the left side of the chart with energy intensity values ranging from roughly 0.5 to 2.0 kWh per dollar. In contrast, wealthy nations like Norway, Denmark, and Australia appear on the far right, showing high GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and low energy intensity below 1.5 kWh per dollar. Several outliers deviate from this general trend, displaying high energy intensity despite varying levels of income. Turkmenistan sits at the top of the chart with the highest energy intensity, exceeding 4.5 kWh per dollar, while North Korea and Bahrain also show values above 4.0 kWh per dollar. Venezuela and Zimbabwe occupy the upper-middle section with intensity levels around 2.3 to 4.3 kWh per dollar. The metric is expressed in international dollars at 2011 prices. The highest values for energy intensity coincide with specific resource-rich or isolated economies, while the lowest values cluster among high-income European and Oceanian nations.
